topics.title: Pronouns · sein, haben, werden
topics.summary: The building blocks plus the three essential irregular verbs.

blocks.p: Personal pronouns (subject): ich, du, er/sie/es, wir, ihr, sie/Sie. 'Sie' (formal you) is always capitalised.

topics.title: Present tense
topics.summary: Regular -en endings plus the vowel-changing irregular stems.

blocks.p: Drop -en from the infinitive and add endings to the stem (lernen → lern-):

blocks.tip: Stems ending in -t/-d add an extra -e- in du/er/ihr: du arbeitest, er findet.

blocks.p: Some common verbs change their stem vowel in the du / er-sie-es forms:

topics.title: du, ihr or Sie?
topics.summary: Choosing the right 'you' — informal vs formal.

blocks.p: du = one person you know well (friends, family, children). ihr = several people you know well. Sie = formal, for one or several people (strangers, officials, shops). Always capitalise Sie.

blocks.tip: In Bavaria you'll also hear 'Servus' and 'Grüß Gott' — but stick to Sie with people you don't know.

topics.title: Word order (verb second)
topics.summary: The conjugated verb loves position 2.

blocks.p: In a main clause the conjugated verb is always the second element. If something other than the subject comes first, the subject moves to just after the verb.

ex: I'm going to the cinema today.
Today I'm going to the cinema.

blocks.tip: Order of extra info: Time – Manner – Place (TeKaMoLo). 'Ich fahre morgen mit dem Bus nach München.'

topics.title: Asking questions
topics.summary: Yes/no inversion, W-questions, and which/how-many.

blocks.p: Yes/no question: put the verb first. 'Kommst du?' 'Hast du Zeit?'

ex: Was für ein Auto ist das?
What kind of car is that?

topics.title: Negation: nicht vs kein
topics.summary: Two ways to say no — pick by what you're negating.

blocks.p: nicht negates a verb, adjective or whole sentence. kein negates a noun (it replaces ein and 'some').

topics.title: Articles & gender
topics.summary: der/die/das, ein/eine, kein — plus suffix shortcuts.

blocks.p: Definite (the): der (masc), die (fem), das (neut), die (plural). Indefinite (a): ein (masc/neut), eine (fem). Negative: kein/keine.
Gender is mostly memorised, but endings hint strongly:

ex: → der (der Montag, der Mai, der Sommer)

topics.title: Plurals
topics.summary: No single rule — but common families.

blocks.p: Plurals are learned per word, but patterns help: -e (der Tisch → die Tische), -er + umlaut (das Buch → die Bücher), -(e)n (die Frau → die Frauen), -s (das Auto → die Autos), or no change (der Lehrer → die Lehrer).

blocks.tip: All plurals take die in the nominative, and add -n in the dative plural: mit den Kindern.

topics.title: The four cases
topics.summary: Nominativ, Akkusativ, Dativ, Genitiv — with the full article tables.

blocks.p: Case shows a noun's job. The article changes; the noun itself usually doesn't (except dative plural +n and genitive +(e)s).

blocks.tip: Only the masculine changes in the accusative (der → den, ein → einen). Open any noun card to see its full declension.

topics.title: Accusative (direct object)
topics.summary: The thing being acted on. Only masculine changes.

blocks.p: Used for the direct object, and after these prepositions (always accusative): durch, für, gegen, ohne, um.

topics.title: Dative (indirect object)
topics.summary: The recipient — and a fixed set of prepositions.

blocks.p: Definite: dem (masc/neut), der (fem), den + noun-n (plural). Always dative after: mit, bei, von, zu, aus, nach, seit, gegenüber.

ex: Ich gebe dem Kind das Buch.
I give the child the book.

topics.title: Pronouns in accusative & dative
topics.summary: me, you, him … and to me, to you, to him.

topics.title: Possessives
topics.summary: mein, dein, sein … take ein-endings.

blocks.p: mein, dein, sein, ihr, unser, euer, ihr/Ihr. They follow the ein-pattern for gender and case.

topics.title: es gibt (there is / are)
topics.summary: One handy phrase, always with the accusative.

blocks.p: 'es gibt' = there is / there are. It never changes form and is always followed by the accusative.

topics.title: Modal verbs
topics.summary: können, müssen, wollen … verb 2nd, infinitive last.

blocks.p: The modal sits in position 2; the main verb goes to the end as an infinitive.

topics.title: Likes & wishes: mögen, möchten, gern
topics.summary: How to say what you like and would like.

blocks.p: mögen = to like (something). möchten = would like (polite wish). gern + verb = to like doing something.

blocks.tip: Preference ladder: gern → lieber → am liebsten. 'Ich trinke lieber Tee.' = I prefer tea.

topics.title: Imperative (commands)
topics.summary: Telling someone to do something.

blocks.tip: e→i stem-changers keep the change (nehmen → Nimm!), but a→ä verbs drop the umlaut (fahren → Fahr!).

topics.title: Separable verbs
topics.summary: The prefix flies to the end of the clause.

blocks.p: Verbs like aufstehen, einkaufen, anrufen split in the present tense — the prefix moves to the end.

blocks.tip: In the Perfekt the prefix rejoins around -ge-: aufgestanden, eingekauft, angerufen.

topics.title: Perfekt tense
topics.summary: The everyday past: haben/sein + past participle.

blocks.p: haben or sein (position 2) + past participle (at the end). Participles: regular ge-…-t (gemacht), irregular ge-…-en (gegessen, getrunken).
Use sein for movement and change of state (gehen, fahren, kommen, bleiben, aufstehen); haben for everything else.

topics.title: Adjective endings
topics.summary: Endings shift after der-words vs ein-words.

blocks.p: An adjective before a noun takes an ending. After a definite article (der/die/das) the endings are 'weak':
After an indefinite article (ein/kein/mein) the adjective carries more info ('mixed'):

ex: der alte Mann / ein alter Mann
das kleine Kind / ein kleines Kind

blocks.tip: With no article the adjective takes the der-endings itself: kalter Kaffee, frische Milch.

topics.title: Comparison (bigger, biggest)
topics.summary: Comparative -er and superlative am …sten.

blocks.p: Comparative: adjective + -er (often + umlaut). Superlative: am + adjective + -sten. 'als' = than; 'so … wie' = as … as.

ex: München ist so schön wie Wien.
Munich is as nice as Vienna.

topics.title: Adverbs of frequency & time
topics.summary: How often, and when.

blocks.p: Frequency: immer (always), oft (often), manchmal (sometimes), selten (rarely), nie (never). They usually come right after the verb.
Time words: heute (today), morgen (tomorrow), gestern (yesterday), jetzt (now), bald (soon), früh/spät (early/late).

topics.title: Conjunctions
topics.summary: Joining clauses — and what they do to word order.

blocks.p: Coordinating conjunctions (und, oder, aber, denn, sondern) sit between two main clauses and do NOT change word order — the verb stays second.

ex: Ich bleibe zu Hause, denn ich bin müde.
I'm staying home, because I'm tired.
Ich trinke Tee, aber er trinkt Kaffee.
I drink tea, but he drinks coffee.

blocks.p: Subordinating conjunctions (weil, dass, wenn, ob) send the verb to the very end of their clause.

ex: Ich bleibe zu Hause, weil ich müde bin.
Ich weiß, dass du Recht hast.

topics.title: Two-way prepositions
topics.summary: Location vs. direction decides the case.

blocks.p: an, auf, hinter, in, neben, über, unter, vor, zwischen take the dative for location (where?) and the accusative for direction (where to?).

ex: I go into the café. (direction → accusative)

topics.title: Prepositions of time
topics.summary: am, um, im and friends.

ex: At the weekend I sleep in.

topics.title: Numbers
topics.summary: Counting, and the famous reversed teens.

blocks.p: 0 null, 1 eins, 2 zwei, 3 drei, 4 vier, 5 fünf, 6 sechs, 7 sieben, 8 acht, 9 neun, 10 zehn, 11 elf, 12 zwölf.
From 21, German says the units first: 21 = einundzwanzig (one-and-twenty), 34 = vierunddreißig. Hundreds: 100 hundert, 1000 tausend.
Ordinals (1st, 2nd …): add -te up to 19, -ste from 20. Irregular: erste (1.), dritte (3.), siebte (7.), achte (8.).

topics.title: Telling the time
topics.summary: Watch out — halb means half TO the next hour!

blocks.p: Es ist … Uhr. For minutes: 'nach' (past) and 'vor' (to). 'Viertel' = quarter.

blocks.tip: 'halb' counts toward the NEXT hour: halb drei = 2:30, not 3:30. Officially you can also just say 'vierzehn Uhr dreißig'.

topics.title: Days, months & dates
topics.summary: All masculine — der Montag, der Mai.

blocks.p: Days, months and seasons are all masculine (der). Seasons: der Frühling, der Sommer, der Herbst, der Winter.
